coredata.dc:description: Nanomaterials based on carbon are becoming increasingly popular due to their adaptability, affordability, and quantity of sources. Among these, carbon dots (C-dots) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are well-known for their contributions to bioimaging and sensing applications. Carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) have high photostability and less toxicity. Thus, it can be observed that fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (FCNPs) emit a powerful green fluorescence that may be used for the bioimaging of cells and in vitro tissues. The study aims to examine the potential effectiveness of FCNPs synthesized utilizing honey-mediated PEG and their antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, cytotoxic, and embryonic toxicological properties. The FCNPs were synthesized using honey and PEG by the heat-induced method. The synthesized FCNPs were characterized using UV absorbance, FT-IR (Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy), EDX (Energy Dispersive X-ray analysis), and SEM (Scanning Electron Microscope) analysis. The synthesized FCNPs were investigated for evaluating antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, cytotoxic, and embryonic toxicology using zebrafish embryos. The synthesized FCNPs exhibited a maximum absorption peak at 300 nm, and the shape was found to be spherical using SEM analysis. The results indicate that the synthesized FCNPs show promising antioxidant, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory activity. Additionally, the toxicological effects of the FCNPs were examined, and they were shown to have less toxicity. In conclusion, finding novel therapeutic agents with widespread applications in biomedicine can be made possible by the sustainable approach of producing FCNPs utilizing honey and PEG as therapeutic agents in the field of biomedical applications.

head.ce:title: Green preparation of fluorescence carbon nanoparticles using honey and its biomedical applications- an in vitro study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanomaterials based on carbon are becoming increasingly popular due to their adaptability, affordability, and quantity of sources. Among these, carbon dots (C-dots) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are well-known for their contributions to bioimaging and sensing applications. Carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) have high photostability and less toxicity. Thus, it can be observed that fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (FCNPs) emit a powerful green fluorescence that may be used for the bioimaging of cells and in vitro tissues. The study aims to examine the potential effectiveness of FCNPs synthesized utilizing honey-mediated PEG and their antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, cytotoxic, and embryonic toxicological properties. The FCNPs were synthesized using honey and PEG by the heat-induced method. The synthesized FCNPs were characterized using UV absorbance, FT-IR (Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy), EDX (Energy Dispersive X-ray analysis), and SEM (Scanning Electron Microscope) analysis. The synthesized FCNPs were investigated for evaluating antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, cytotoxic, and embryonic toxicology using zebrafish embryos. The synthesized FCNPs exhibited a maximum absorption peak at 300 nm, and the shape was found to be spherical using SEM analysis. The results indicate that the synthesized FCNPs show promising antioxidant, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory activity. Additionally, the toxicological effects of the FCNPs were examined, and they were shown to have less toxicity. In conclusion, finding novel therapeutic agents with widespread applications in biomedicine can be made possible by the sustainable approach of producing FCNPs utilizing honey and PEG as therapeutic agents in the field of biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The Scientific study of life processes, factors that affect health, disease prevention, and treatment comes under biomedical research. The main objective of the various disciplines in biomedical research focus on molecular and cellular mechanisms of disease and to develop new diagnostics and therapies [1]. The different sectors of biomedical sciences include medical imaging, drug delivery systems, development of biomaterials, cancer research, genetic analysis, tissue engineering, regenerative medicine, diagnostics, personalized medicine, infectious disease treatment, and the study of disease mechanisms using various techniques like cell culture, animal models, and clinical trials [2]. The development of new materials, especially nanoparticles and nanocomposites with unique characteristics, have been utilized in various industries like health, food, cosmetics, and agriculture [3]. Research on the environmentally friendly production of different types of nanoparticles becomes more essential, especially due to the preservation of the environment and ethical growth. This method makes use of biological substances, such as plant extracts, to generate nanoparticles in an economical, effective, and ecologically sustainable way [4]. The size, structure, and stability of the acquired metallic nanoparticles can be determined using characterization techniques such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning

Electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and turbidimetry [5]. Recent research has shown promising results in many possible ways related to health. However, compared to other nanoparticles, carbon nanoparticles have specific advanced physio-chemical properties, which are synthesized using the green synthesis method [6].

Graphene nanosheets, nanofibers, fullerenes, nanocrystals, and porous carbon compounds are examples of carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) that hold significant potential for a range of uses in drug delivery, electrochemistry, biosensors, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (FCNPs) in bio-imaging, tissue regeneration, and microelectronic devices [7]. A variety of techniques have been used to develop CNPs, including the candle soot method, laser-induced pyrolysis of hydrocarbons, low temperature solution synthesis, electro-chemical oxidation of graphite, microwave pyrolysis of sucrose, proton-beam irradiation of nanodiamonds, thermal breakdown of organic substances, the use of mesoporous fragments of silica as templates, and the use of polyacrylonitrile (PAN) as an indicator to create elements. Despite different methods attainable for the synthesis of CNPs, the microwave heat method of carbon quantum dot achieved [8], carbon dots using hydrothermal method using plant extract [9] pyrolysis of sucrose and candle soot are the most practical ways due to their cost viability [10]. Because of their strong antibacterial properties and high thermal efficiency, carbon-based nanomaterials are especially ideal for applications in biomedicine and food packaging [11]. There has been a lot of significance in CNPs among researchers along with certain physiologically attractive qualities like biodegradability, low toxicology, and minimal adverse effects on the environment, these substances also have attractive qualities including photoluminescence, chemo and photostability, high solubility in water, ease of synthesis, and surface functionalization [12]. The advantages of CNPs include their easy and hassle-free synthesis, low cost, biocompatibility, superior photo-luminescence (PL) characteristics, and high stability in a variety of systems [13]. Because of their abundant surface area, non-toxicity, and ease of low-cost synthesis using eco-friendly techniques, carbon-based nanoparticles are being examined for the development of advanced materials [14].

Honey is a natural sweetener that has been used for a while in nutritional products and natural medicines. It is primarily made up of fructose and glucose, with smaller amounts of minerals, amino acids, vitamins, and phytochemical active compounds [15]. The pleasant natural nectar known as monofloral honey is in great supply on the global market [16]. Honey is made from nectar, honeydew, or a mixture of bee-collected nectar with bee secretions, possessing high nutritional values [17]. Honey is a naturally occurring food item that provides health and nutritional benefits as a great source of organic acids, minerals, carbohydrates, and amino acids. The anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antioxidant qualities of honey are associated with phenolic compounds. In addition, they can serve as flag markers to distinguish between various honey types [18]. One of the most natural sweetening mixtures, honey comprises sugars as well as other organic compounds and components that have unique nutraceutical qualities for human consumption and a variety of other pharmacological applications. The natural world exhibits intricate relationships between living things, and bees use plants in the Amaranthaceae family to produce honey. These plants transfer certain molecules to the honey, which likely has medicinal or nutraceutical benefits [19]. On the other side, the most prominent polymer for enhancing nanoparticles' in-vivo characteristics is polyethylene glycol (PEG). For more than 20 years, PEG-modified (PEGylated) liposomes were utilized in clinical treatment for cancer due to their increased permeability and retention effect, which allows them to circulate in the blood for an extended period and gain richness in tumors. PEG can be utilized to alter EVs without or with a minor impact on EV shape and size distribution due to its structural similarity to liposomes [20]. PEG penetrates the atmosphere in significant amounts, more than two metric megatons annually, and is comparatively chemically stable. PEG is known as a xenobiotic since it lacks an identical structural contrast in nature [21]. In several organizations, the most often utilized colonic cleanser and astringent is the polyethylene glycol (PEG) electrolyte solution [22]. To increase solubility, efficiency, storage, transportation, and security, dispersing agents such as polyethylene glycol (PEG) and polyoxyethylene lauryl ether (PLE) have been used as surfactants that are non-ionic in residential and biocidal applications [23]. Due to its biological compatibility, stability in a variety of solvents, and potential to form stable complexes, polyethylene glycol (PEG-6000) is a high molecular weight polymer with numerous uses in materials research, coatings, and therapeutics. PEG-6000 is involved in complex molecular reactions when dissolved in solvents like ethanol, which significantly affect the solution's density, viscosity, and acoustic qualities [24]. PEG is used in an extensive variety of industries, including pharmaceutical, cosmetic, and medical. It can be used as a lubrication or anti-freezing agent and to eliminate heavy metals. PEG's universal existence in industrial and urban water effluents raises concerns regarding possible environmental effects [25].

CNPs have been playing an important role in biomedical applications because of their high surface area, excellent electrical conductivity, good biocompatibility, the ability to be easily functionalized, and potential for targeted delivery. Most of the nanoparticles exhibit maximum toxicity. To develop a carbon nanoparticle by using one pot - microwave synthesis method and reducing the toxicity of the nanoparticle is the major aim of the research. In this study, FCNPs were synthesized using honey and PEG by the heat-induced method. The synthesized FCNPs were checked for UV absorbance using a multi-beam spectrophotometer and other characterization techniques, including FT-IR, SEM, and EDX. H2O2 (Hydrogen peroxide scavenging activity), DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl), and FRAP (Ferric Reducing Antioxidant Power Assay) were used to test the antioxidant properties of synthesized FCNPs. The anti-inflammatory effects were investigated using EA (Egg albumin denaturation assay), BSA (Bovine serum albumin assay), and MSA (Membrane stabilization assay), and the antimicrobial properties of FCNPs were assessed by agar well diffusion method. Furthermore, FCNPs are tested for their cytotoxic effect using BSLA (Brine shrimp lethality assay), and embryonic toxicology evaluation was performed using zebrafish embryos.

To synthesize FCNPs, honey (100 % pure) was collected from marthandam Indian honey (Herbal store), and Polyethylene glycol 99 % polyethylene glycol was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Absolute Ethanol 99 % and Whatman paper (2.5 μm) purchased from GE Healthcare Care Life Sciences were utilized for the filtration and purification process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of honey and PEG-mediated FCNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Honey is collected from the herbal store, and PEG was utilized for the synthesis of FCNPs. 5 g of pure honey & 1 g of PEG purchased from Sigma Aldrich were mixed and kept in the crucible. The crucible containing the mixture of honey and PEG was kept in a microwave oven at 700 V for 1 h. After 1 h, the residues settled in the crucible were scraped well and washed with a prepared ethanol solution (5:15) of distilled water two times. The collected sample was filtered through the Whatman filter paper. The supernatant collected was checked for its fluorescence. The pellet was collected and ground into fine powder, followed by using an ultra-probe sonicator for 4 times at 5 min intervals. The FCNPs were kept in a hot air oven for drying and used for further studies.

In this study, the extremely efficient FCNPs were synthesized using a microwave oven in a single step. The UV absorbance of the synthesized FCNPs process was then investigated using a multibeam spectrophotometer with a wavelength range of 250–650 nm. Further characterizations were carried out by FT-IR analysis, SEM, EDX, and TEM micrography.

The antibacterial activity was evaluated using the agar well diffusion technique. To ensure optimal development, newly inoculated pathogens, such as Pseudomonas sp., E. coli, E. faecalis, and S. aureus, were put into the sterile broth and incubated for 24 h at 130–150 rpm using an orbital shaker. Muller Hinton agar plates were made by standard procedures. The antibacterial activity of FCNPs synthesized using honey and PEG at different concentrations against wound pathogens. A gel puncher was used to create four wells, and a sterile swab was used to swab the organisms. The first three wells were filled with the three distinct concentrations (25 μL, 50 μL, and 100 μg/mL), while the fourth well was filled with the standard (Amoxyrite) at a concentration of 15 μg/mL and the plates were observed after 24 h of incubation. The zone of inhibition was noted [26].

This experiment evaluated the FCNPs' ability to scavenge hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) free radicals. Using 50 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) and 0.6 mL of H2O2 solution (2 mM), the volume was increased to 0.4 mL after an aliquot of 0.1 mL of FCNPs (10–50 μg/mL) was transferred into the Eppendorf tubes. The absorbance at 230 nm was determined during the reaction mixture's vortexing and 10 min of reaction time. In this experiment, ascorbic acid served as the positive control. The previous literature [27] was referred to establish the percentage of inhibition.

A stock solution of 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) at a concentration of 0.1 mM was prepared using methanol. To make a fresh working solution for each test, dilute the stock solution with methanol until the final concentration reaches 20 μM. In a 96-well plate, different quantities of honey and PEG-mediated FCNPs (10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 μg/mL) were combined with 200 μL of DPPH solution. The plate was incubated for 30 min at room temperature in the dark, and a microplate reader was used to measure the absorbance at 517 nm. Ascorbic acid was employed as a standard reference, and the percentage of inhibition was assessed by previous studies [28].

An antioxidant power test utilizing ferric reduction was used to identify the antioxidants present in the FCNPs and the amount of Fe+3 to Fe+2 that they reduced. First, 3.6 mL of an antioxidant power solution with ferric reduction were employed. Next, add 0.4 mL of distilled water. Following the addition of FCNPs (10–50 μg/mL), the mixture was left in a dark area for 10 min. According to the previous research, the absorbance was measured at 593 nm at five different dosages of FeSO4, 7H2O of FCNPs in FRAP reagent [29].

0.2 mL of fresh egg albumin is mixed with 2.8 mL of phosphate buffer to initiate the egg albumin denaturation experiment. PEG-mediated CNPs and honey were added to the reaction mixture at different quantities (10–50 μg/mL). The pH was maintained below 6.3. The sample was incubated in a water bath at 55 °C for 30 min after 10 min at room temperature. Diclofenac sodium was employed as the standard, according to Ref. [30]. At 660 nm, spectrophotometric analysis was performed on the samples.

The administration of FCNPs (10–50 μg/mL) to 0.45 mL of bovine serum albumin was done. The pH was adjusted to 6.3. After 10 min at ambient temperature, it was incubated for 30 min at 55 °C in a water bath. The standard used was diclofenac sodium. After that, the samples were analyzed at 660 nm using a multibeam spectrophotometer [31].

To create an RBC suspension, the blood was collected from a healthy human volunteer who had not taken any NSAIDS for 2 weeks before the experiment and collected. Centrifugation was used to separate the red blood cells from the blood at 1000 rotations per minute (RPM). The RBCs were cleaned with PBS after the liquid component had been discarded. Reconstituted RBCs should have a 10 % (v/v) RBC suspension in the tris-HCl buffer. Using a pipette, pour 1 mL of the RBC suspension into each centrifuge tube. Subsequently, varying amounts of FCNPs (10–50 μg/mL) were introduced into every tube. After thoroughly blending the mixture, it was centrifuged once more for 10 min at 1000 RPM. Using a UV–vis spectrophotometer, the supernatant's absorbance at 540 nm was measured [32].

200 mL of filtered water should be used to dissolve 2 g of iodine-free salt. Ten to 12 mL of saline water were applied to six well ELISA plates. At different concentrations of (5 μL, 10 μL, 20 μL, 40 μL, and 80 μL) ten nauplii were carefully added to each well. The sample-loaded plates were incubated for 24 h. The number of live nauplii may be calculated using the following formula after the ELISA plates have been checked for 24 h [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zebrafish embryonic toxicology evaluation of FCNPs
ce:section.ce:para: An AB strain of zebrafish (Danio rerio) maintained in the Saveetha Medical College and Hospital's Nano biomedicine lab provided the zebrafish. Zebrafish were maintained in individual tanks with controlled pH (6.5–8.5), light/dark cycle (14:10 h), and temperature (280 ± 20C). The most nutritious diet was given to the fish twice a day. Groups of zebrafish were raised, and live eggs were collected. The freshly made E3 medium was used to wash the collected eggs at least three times. On culture plates with different well sizes (6, 12, and 24), fertilized eggs were stacked. FCNPs were added to healthy embryos 4–72 h after fertilization at doses ranging from 5 to 80 μg/mL. The embryos were incubated in E3 media that had been combined with FCNPs. Furthermore, control groups were included in the trial. Every 12 h, dead embryos from the groups exposed to nanoparticles were taken out. All test plates were maintained at 28 °C and covered with foil to prevent light from getting in.

After fertilization, zebrafish embryos were exposed, and their developmental phases were studied under a stereo microscope. Embryos were exposed to several doses of FCNPs (5, 10, 20, 40, and 80 μg/mL) over the 4–72 hpf period. Every 24 h, the rates of viability and hatching were recorded. The purpose of this study was to evaluate the viability and hatching rate of the embryos as well as note any abnormalities in the embryos and larvae in the treatment and control groups. The embryos were observed using a COSLAB HL-10A light microscope, and the proportion of damaged embryos was recorded every 24 h [34].

To ensure that the results of the study are accurate, each experiment has been performed in triplicate. The statistical analysis of the observed anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, antimicrobial activity, cytotoxic impact, and embryonic toxicity using zebra fish embryos was conducted using the standard error (SE) method. The standard error provides a measure of the variability of the sample means and allows for an assessment of the correctness of the results. The test samples and standard drug concentrations were assessed using a significance criterion of p < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of honey & PEG mediated FCNPS and its fluorescence test
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of FCNPs was visually confirmed by a change in the color of the reaction mixture when a microwave oven was employed. Microwave heating caused a gradual color shift in the reactants from yellow to yellowish brown. The overall synthesis process is shown in Fig. 1 (a–d). The fluorescence effect of FCNPs was tested using a UV transilluminator and compared with distilled water. The one-step synthesized FCNPs were analyzed under 254 nm short wavelength UV light. The microwave-assisted FCNPs exhibited a visible fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 1e-g. Thus, the synthesized FCNPs can be used in Bioimaging applications.

The FT-IR results of honey show different types of stretches at various peak points were observed; 3351.113 represents O-H stretching; 2927.550 exhibits C-H stretching, 1704.547 shows C=O stretching, and 1614.111 represents C=C stretching. The weaker band was observed at 1640.712 representing the C=C group and the sharp peak at 1015.818 exhibits C-F stretching - fluoro compound as shown in Fig. 2 a. Comparatively, the synthesized FCNPs' FTIR spectra showed many distinctive peaks that each corresponded to a distinct functional group. The stretching vibrations of hydroxyl (–OH) groups are responsible for the large absorption peak in the region of 1412.555, which suggests the existence of surface hydrophilic functional groups. The secondary amine, >N-H bend, which correlates to the peak at 2930.748, suggests that the surface of FCNPs contains carbonyl functionalities. Additional peaks were seen at 3280.714 representing C-H stretching; 2930.748 shows N-H stretching; 1640.712 exhibits C=C stretching; 1412.555 with O-H bending; 1023.475 showing C-N stretching; and 869.229 indicates C-H bending. The oxidation and functionalization activities that occur during synthesis are responsible for the presence of these functional groups on the surface of FCNPs, which is shown in Fig. 2b. These functional groups provide the nanoparticles with hydrophilicity and make surface modification and bioconjugation easier for specific purposes. Furthermore, by affecting the quantum yield and fluorescence emission of FCNPs, the surface functional groups are critical to their optical characteristics. The FCNPs' beneficial absorption of UV light was demonstrated by the notable absorption peak in their UV–Vis absorption spectra, which was observed at around 300 nm, as shown in Fig. 2c. The existence of π-π∗ transitions in the carbonaceous structure of the nanoparticles is responsible for this absorption behaviour. The presence of surface functional groups and sp2 carbon domains on the FCNPs surface, which promote effective radiative recombination of excitons, is most likely the cause of the observed fluorescence. These findings demonstrate FCNPs' potential for use in UV-related applications, such as UV imaging probes and sensors.

Significant peaks for the elements carbon (C), oxygen (O), and calcium (Ca) were visible in the FCNPs' EDX spectra. The element that accounted for the bulk of the elemental makeup was discovered to be carbon, which is consistent with the nanoparticles' carbonaceous origin. The occurrence of oxygen peaks indicates either the surface functional groups containing oxygen atoms or the oxidation of carbon during synthesis. The elemental composition study indicates that FCNPs are mostly composed of carbon and provides important insights into their chemical nature. The effective synthesis of FCNPs is shown by the EDX analysis, which verified the elemental composition of the FCNPs, accounting for around 69.2 % carbon, 27.9 % oxygen, and 2.8 % calcium. It shows that FCNPs were successfully synthesized. Since surface functional groups like hydroxyl and carboxyl groups may be present when oxygen is present, this might have an impact on the surface characteristics and how FCNPs interact with biological systems. The EDX analysis of FCNPs is shown in Fig. 3 .

The scanning electron microscope operates on the principle of kinetic energy, which generates signals based on electron interactions. These electrons include secondary electrons, diffracted backscattered electrons, and backscattered electrons. They are used to observe photons and crystallized components. Secondary electrons and backscattered electrons are used to produce a picture. The secondary electrons emitted from the material are primarily responsible for determining its topography and shape, whereas the backscattered electrons disclose variations in the components' composition. The shape and size distribution of the synthesized FCNPs were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The SEM scans revealed that the nanoparticles have a uniform spherical shape with a comparatively narrow size dispersion, as seen in Fig. 4 a and b.

The HR-TEM analysis was conducted to investigate detailed insights into the size and morphology of the biosynthesized FCNPs developed using honey and PEG, as shown in Fig. 5 a and b. The developed FCNPs were mostly small and large spherical and polydisperse, and aggregation of the FCNPS was noted. The minimum and maximum particle size of the FCNPs was found to be 8.59–58.695 nm, and the average size of the FCNPs was 19.94995 nm. A Histogram of the particle size distribution of biosynthesized FCNPs is represented in Fig. 5c.

E. coli, S. aureus, Pseudomonas sp, and E. faecalis were some of the wound pathogens used to investigate the antibacterial activity of FCNPs synthesized with an environmentally friendly technique. Notably, FCNPs exhibited a minimal zone of inhibition for all the tested wound pathogens. The findings show that FCNPs had a 10 mm zone of inhibition and showed strong antibacterial efficacy against S. aureus at a concentration of 100 μg/mL. By contrast, FCNPs had less action against E. Coli, with a 9 mm zone. Across all examined microorganisms, the standard consistently showed the highest zones of inhibition, confirming the assay's effectiveness. The synthesized FCNPs appear to have strong antibacterial capabilities against wound infections, based on these findings. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 , we used a two-way ANOVA test to find that the experimental p-value was less than 0.05, indicating considerable antibacterial activity at 100 μg/mL.

The antioxidant activity of ascorbic acid and synthesized FCNPs against H2O2 radicals in an H2O2 experiment is displayed in the bar graph in Fig. 7 a. FCNPs display 74.3 % inhibition at 50 μg/mL, compared to 89.8 % for the standard. The standard exhibits an inhibition percentage of 51.5 % at 10 μg/mL, while the FCNPs demonstrate an equivalent inhibition rate of 44.4 %.

In this study, different concentrations (10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 μg/mL) were investigated to assess the efficacy of FCNPs in scavenging free radicals. The DPPH experiment showed that the quantity of FCNPs and their capacity to scavenge free radicals interacted in a dose-dependent manner. The concentration of FCNPs was shown to considerably boost the inhibition of DPPH radicals. The results of the study indicate that FCNPs greatly lower the antioxidant capacity of DPPH. 66.25 % at 10 μg/mL, 78.52 % at 20 μg/mL, 85.63 % at 30 μg/mL, 88.68 % at 40 μg/mL, and 93.15 % at 50 μg/mL are the values that the standard provides. In contrast, 53.6 % of the synthesized FCNPs exhibit inhibition at 10 μg/mL, 67.3 % at 20 μg/mL, 71.9 % at 30 μg/mL, 77.5 % at 40 μg/mL, and 83.3 % at 50 μg/mL was observed and represented in Fig. 7b.

Using the FRAP test, Fig. 7c illustrates the antioxidant activity of honey and PEG-mediated FCNPs. Ferric ions are changed into ferrous ions by the chemical. The FCNPs show 81.28 % at 50 μg/mL, while the standard ascorbic acid shows 90.89 % at that concentration. Synthesized FCNPs demonstrated 60.37 % at 10 μg/mL, while traditional ascorbic acid demonstrated 72.98 %. This indicates that the dosage was the only factor affecting the drug's effectiveness.

Fig. 8 a shows that honey and PEG-mediated CNPs inhibited egg albumin denaturation at different concentrations. Diclofenac sodium, an effective anti-inflammatory medical practice, has a stronger ability to prevent EA denaturation across all concentrations. At 10 μg/mL, honey and PEG-mediated CNPs exhibited 49 % inhibition, whereas diclofenac sodium inhibited 55 %. At 20 μg/mL, honey and PEG-mediated CNPs inhibited at 58 % and diclofenac sodium at 64 %, respectively. At 30 μg/mL, they were 61 % and 69 %, respectively. At 40 μg/mL, they were 68 % and 72 %, respectively. At the maximum concentration of 50 μg/mL, they were 77 % and 81 %.

The BSA test was used to evaluate the anti-inflammatory activity at five different reaction mixture concentrations (10–50 μg/mL). Tests for anti-inflammatory efficacy showed inhibitions of 44 %, 54 %, 66 %, 69.7 %, and 73.2 %, in that order. On the other hand, the standard solution showed a maximal inhibition of 47 % at a concentration of 10 μg/mL and 84 % at a dose of 50 μL. Moreover, it demonstrates that PEG-mediated FCNPs and honey had the strongest anti-inflammatory effects, as demonstrated by Fig. 8b.

The MSA test was employed to assess the anti-inflammatory properties of FCNPs. The inhibitory effects of the FCNPs were compared to standard values after they were tested at various doses. The findings indicated that at 10 μg/mL, there was a 51 % inhibition, and at 50 μg/mL, a 78 % inhibition. In comparison, the standard diclofenac sodium demonstrated 58 % and 89 % inhibition at 10 μg/mL and 50 μg/mL, respectively. In particular, it was shown that at all tested concentrations, the anti-inflammatory properties of FCNPs were equivalent to those of normal diclofenac sodium. The anti-inflammatory effects of the standard (diclofenac sodium) and the one-step synthesized FCNPs are compared in the graph presented in Fig. 8c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxic effect using brine shrimp lethality assay
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxic impact of honey and PEG-mediated CNPs was assessed using a brine shrimp lethality test on Artemia salina. Various quantities (5–80 μg/mL) of CNPs were tested on brine shrimps. The synthesized CNPs were cross-validated and compared with at 5 μL, 100 % of live nauplii were detected, whereas at 10 μL, 100 % of live nauplii were observed. The 20 μL concentration resulted in 90 % live nauplii, while the 40 μL concentration yielded 90 %. A maximum concentration of 80 μL resulted in 80 % of viable nauplii, while the control group had 100 %, as shown in Fig. 9 c. Fig. 9a represents the control group, and Fig. 9b represents the FCNPs-loaded images of brine shrimp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of embryonic toxicology of FCNPs using zebrafish embryos
ce:section.ce:para: In the experiment, hatching rates from 0 hpf to 72 hpf were not substantially affected by exposing zebrafish embryos to FCNPs at doses ranging from 5 to 80 μg/mL. On the other hand, hatching was significantly affected by doses of 40 and 80 μg/mL, which is observed as 90 % and 80 % of hatching occurred at 72 hpf, respectively. The different quantities of FCNPs were discovered to be 80 μg/mL, and no development-related problems were seen at any of the concentrations (Fig. 10 a). These findings imply that the synthesized FCNPs were generally less harmful to zebrafish embryos. The viability rate of FCNPs was evaluated between 0 and 72 hpf. The results demonstrated a consistent viability rate at all tested concentrations (5–80 μg/mL) when compared to the control group, indicating low toxicity. Conversely, it was shown that the concentrations of 40 and 80 μg/mL FCNPs had a substantial effect on the viability rate (p < 0.0005), resulting in 80 and 70 % viability by 72 hpf, respectively. The findings imply that lower FCNPs concentrations show less toxicity, as shown in Fig. 10b. The zebrafish embryos were treated with varying concentrations of FCNPs (5–80 μg/mL) before 4 h post-fertilization (hpf). The rate at which the embryos hatch is progressively impacted by an increase in FCNPs concentration. When FCNPs were added at concentrations of 5–20 μg/mL, the hatching rate of the embryos was 100 %; at 40 μg/mL, it was 90 %; and at 80 μg/mL, it was 80 %. In this investigation, no abnormalities were found at the highest concentration, which is represented in Fig. 10(f–h) and the control group was shown in Fig. 10(c–e), which exhibits less toxicity of the developed FCNPs.

In this study, the FCNPs were synthesized using honey and PEG by a microwave-assisted method. The one-step synthesized FCNPs were characterized using UV, FT-IR, EDX, and SEM. Further, the synthesized FCNPs were tested for their antimicrobial properties, Antioxidant effect, Anti-inflammatory properties, cytotoxic effect, and embryonic toxicology using zebrafish embryos. Overall, research results indicate that FCNPs that have been synthesized can be used as pharmaceutical agents. In conclusion, much to learn about the diverse functions that FCNPs can play in the management and treatment of disease. They have enormous promise for use in biomedical applications.

Utilizing short-wavelength UV light (254 nm), the fluorescence characteristics of the synthesized CQDs were examined. Under daylight conditions, the generated CQDs had a light brown appearance, but when exposed to UV light, they displayed a green luminescence. Hints regarding the existence of quantum particles in the generated solution were given by the green fluorescence when exposed to UV light as per the previous literature [35]. The hydrothermal reaction of spermidine trihydrochloride was used to create cationic carbon dots, which were then characterized by TEM examination, emission, and UV–visible absorption. The π–π∗ transition of the C=C bond is represented by absorption bands (abs) in the UV–visible absorption spectra of the carbon dots, whereas the n‐π∗ transition of the C=O and C=N bonds are represented by a shoulder band at 300–450 nm. The emission that is n-dependent upon stimulation validates the creation of carbon dots and is linked to particles of varying sizes, surface imperfections, and polycyclic clusters of varying sizes [36]. Following synthesis, and 72–144 h later, a typical set of UV–vis spectra is gathered, with the records made between 200 and 800 nm range. According to experimental results presented for the unreduced nanocarbon dispersion [37], the observed data indicates the presence of two distinct peaks observed at 236 nm and about 310 nm. These locations are probably correlated to the aromatic C-C bond π→π∗ transitions and the C=O bond n→π∗ transitions.

The distinctive absorption bands associated with the −OH stretching vibration phase were detected at approximately 3400 and 1073 cm−1. In the C-H stretching phase, the band at 2923 cm-1 is located. Additionally, the peaks seen at 1590 and 1400 cm−1, respectively, might be accounted for by the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of COO. The hydroxyl and carboxylic groups in the plant came from the same location, according to these results [38].

The bands' respective endpoints were 3457, 3260, 1637, 1371, and 1023 cm-1. These bands demonstrate the functional groups that are present on CD surfaces. The band emerging at 3260 cm-1 correlates with the bending vibrations of C N groups, whereas the band centered at 3457 cm-1 is associated with the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H. C-O-C bonds, CH2, and C-N vibrations are shown by the bands at 1637, 1371, and 1023 cm-1, respectively. Since the carbon dots' surfaces contained amine, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups, the FTIR data showed that they were hydrophilic. It is most likely the hydrophilic surface that provides the remarkable water dispersibility [39].

The Mesoporous Hematite Composite - Carbon Quantum Dots – Spherical [40] Scanning Electron Microscope Image represents the SEM data. The size distribution of spherical CDs is consistent with the Gaussian distribution, and their diameter is around 4.5 nm. The PC's asymmetrical block-like form is visible in the SEM pictures. On the other hand, a high yield of uniform-size and shape nanoparticles was generated in the normally manufactured CDs [41]. The spin coating process's settings were adjusted to create monolayers of the microspheres to study the scattering caused by the microspheres' dispersion. We can find different dispersions in different areas of a film because microsphere dispersions are not entirely uniform in three typical scenarios of the produced microspheres. The observed surface in all of the microspheres covering it. Most of the time, the spheres are densely packed together with sporadic gaps in between. Microspheres cover a significant portion of the region. Aggregations are formed by all of the spheres being in a single layer. Tens of spheres make up each aggregation, and periodicity is rarely discernible [42].

However, the TEM analysis of the CQDS showed Quasi-spherical in shape and average size of (7–8 nm) and the CQDS was observed as amorphous [43]. Comparatively, Mesoporous carbon shows uniformly separated spheres with a diameter of 140 nm, and the average pore size was obtained to be 2.6 nm [44], where the black primary particles were found to be 47 nm [45].

Two processes were involved in the innovative microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon quantum dots from raw polysaccharide material (cashew gum): carbonization in solid and partial depolymerization in solution. The synthesis is quick, simple, economical, and environmentally friendly. An excellent quantum yield photoluminescent nanoparticle was produced with no external passivation or functionalization needed. Based on tests and findings documented in the literature, a two-step mechanism was proposed. The C-dot surface's predicted lack of toxicity and negative charge increases hydrophilicity and stability in an aqueous solution and suggests potential use in in vivo imaging [46]. In previous studies [47], Azadirachta indica leaves were successfully used to synthesize CDs using a renewable green source. The employed sand bath synthesis technique is an economical and quite simple process. The synthetic carbon dots showed excellent excitation-dependent emission, and even at greater concentrations, it was found that they were biocompatible. The CDs also demonstrated strong antioxidant activity, remarkable antibacterial activity, and considerable free radical scavenging activities. This indicates that CDs' antibacterial and antifungal activity showed dose-dependent inhibition. The mechanisms behind growth suppression can be explained by membrane lysis brought on by interactions between the negative charges on the cell surface and the positive charges on CDs.

Antioxidants such as flavonoids and phenolics may contribute to honey's antioxidant activity [48]. Additionally, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles demonstrated strong antioxidant activity by scavenging the free radicals. This characteristic might be explained by the electron transfer from IO-NPs' Fe + complexes. Thus, at a concentration of 1 mg/mL, HHLIO-NPs demonstrated increased radical scavenging of 80 %, while free IO-NPs and HH demonstrated 49 % and 35 %, respectively. Based on the previous studies [49], a standard was used to measure the anti-inflammatory activity of freshly synthesized nanoparticles, and excellent median findings were interpreted. The solution's reduction of BSA inflammation was associated with beneficial results. The freshly synthesized nanoparticles at the lowest concentration (200 μg/ml) had an AI value of 68 % and a value of 549 μg/ml. The concentration of honey employed in the nanoparticle production process was shown to affect the results, as the anti-inflammatory potential of the synthesized particles is dose-dependent. The anti-inflammatory properties of the solution will be enhanced by a sharp increase in the quantity of nanoparticles.

Two eggs per well were added onto the well culture plate at 4 hpf, where the well contains embryo media. GO dispersions at various concentrations (5, 10, 50, and 100 μg·mL-1) and oxidized CNO were added. An embryo media was used as the negative control in the experiment. The eggs were observed for every 12 h. The result findings show that 80 % of the zebrafish survived at the dosages of 50 and 100 μL. Counting the number of viable embryos or larvae at each interval can be used to analyze the survival rate, according to previous research [50].

Nanoparticles can be synthesized using various methods and green synthesis is the majorly preferred method used in biomedical applications. CNPs possess the following properties including High surface area, Electrical conductivity, Biocompatibility, Surface functionalization, Optical properties, Mechanical properties, Drug delivery, Biosensing, Tissue engineering, medical imaging, Photodynamic therapy, etc. Some of the nanoparticles exhibit more toxicity. In the present study, the synthesis of FCNPs, characterization of FCNPs, antimicrobial activity, antioxidant activity, anti-inflammatory activity, cytotoxic effect, and embryonic toxicology were investigated successfully. FCNPs showed fluorescence properties which can be used in bio-imaging applications related to diagnostics. The biosynthesized FCNPs exhibited efficient antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities. The effective antimicrobial activity and very less toxicity were observed during the study. With less toxicity, the use of nanotechnology—more especially, FCNPs—could be one of the greatest ways to combat illness, infection, anticancer treatment, dentistry, coating systems, wound healing, and many other biomedical applications.

Pradeep Manigandan: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Rajeshkumar Shanmugam: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.
